texts.orig: Calculation  methods  for  determining  pure  gas  permeabilities and  gas  separation  performance  of hollow  fiber  modules  with significant permeate pressure drop inside the fiber are presented. A  sequential  optimization method  for  determining  the  nearoptimum  permeate  pressures in  cascade  operation  is  developed. It is shown  that the  performance of narrow hollow fiber modules  can  be  significantly affected  by  the  permeate  pressure build-up  inside  the narrow  fiber. For a given  module and given  recovery  there  exists  an  optimum  permeate  outlet  pressure  which  minimizes  the  membrane  and  permeate  compression  costs.  Examples  given  relate  to  the  recovery  of helium from natural  gas.
texts.text: Calculation  methods  for  determining  pure  gas  permeabilities and  gas  separation  performance  of hollow  fiber  modules  with significant permeate pressure drop inside the fiber are presented. A  sequential  optimization method  for  determining  the  nearoptimum  permeate  pressures in  cascade  operation  is  developed. It is shown  that the  performance of narrow hollow fiber modules  can  be  significantly affected  by  the  permeate  pressure build-up  inside  the narrow  fiber. For a given  module and given  recovery  there  exists  an  optimum  permeate  outlet  pressure  which  minimizes  the  membrane  and  permeate  compression  costs.  Examples  given  relate  to  the  recovery  of helium from natural  gas.

texts.orig: he  calculation  methods  and  analysis  for the  perT meation process  presented  in Part I of  this series are based  on  uniform  feed and permeate  pressures  in the  permeation  stage.  This  is  a  reasonable  assumption for a permeation  module  employing flat sheet or tubular  membranes.  In  a  hollow-fiber module,  however, the narrow fiber bore  (typically 40-100 pm ID) may  give  rise  to  a  significant  pressure  drop.  The effect  of  such  pressure  drop  will  depend  on  whether the  high pressure  feed  flows  inside  or outside  of the  fiber. The permeate/feed  pressure  ratio  which  is perhaps  the  most important  operating  ~ariable'-~', is  more  sensitive  to  the  variation  of  the  permeate pressure  than  the  feed  pressure.  In  the  feed  outside the  fiber  mode,  the  pressure  ratio  may  be  seriously affected by the  permeate  pressure build-up inside the  fiber,  which  can  easily  be  a  significant  fraction of (or  even  greater  than) the  permeate  pressure at  the  fibre  outlet.  For  the  feed  inside  the  fiber mode, the main effect caused by the feed pressure drop is  the  reduced  pressure  difference  across  the  fiber wall. The pressure ratio may not be seriously affected, unless  the  pressure  drop  is  a  significant  fraction  of the feed pressure. The choice of  the mode of  operation will depend  on  the  application.  Generally  speaking, the  feed  flow  outside  the  fiber  mode  is  preferred for a  separation  requiring  only  a small stage cut  (i.e. small  fraction  of feed  permeated) such  as  the  recovery of helium from natural gas  with a high selectivity  membrane.  Here  the  amount  of  feed  gas processed  per  unit volume  of  helium recovered is very large because of  low helium  concentration in the feed. If  the  natural  gas  were  to  flow  inside  of the  fiber, the  large  feed  flow  and  the  attendant  large pressure drop  will  give  rise  to  a  prohibitive  residue  recompression cost. On the other hand,  the feed flow inside  the  fiber  mode  may  minimize  the  effect  of pressure  drop  inside  the  fiber  for  cases  with  large stage cut  (or equivalently low  feed  velocity)  such  as the H? and  CO separation from  synthesis gas.

texts.orig: of  feed  flow  inside the  fiber  have  been  presented  by Thorman,  Rhim  and  H ~ a n g ' ~ ' and  their  theoretical model  was  found  in good  agreement with  the experimental  results.  Recently, Stern, Onorato and Libo~e'~' have  studied  the effect  of  elastic deformation  on  gas permeation  in  externally  pressurized hollow  fibers. But  the  effect  of  permeate  pressure  variation  along the fiber bore was not  taken  into account, because the relatively low permeation flux of  the symmetric fibers used  in  their  study  did  not  give  rise  to  significant pressure build-up.  The calculation for the case of  feed flow inside  the  fiber  is  a  straightforward initial value  problem''.  For  feed  flow  outside  of  the  fiber, however.  the  boundary  conditions  a t   either  end of the  fiber  will  not  be  completely  known (mainly  because the permeate pressure  build-up  inside the fiber is  not  known).  Normally  a  trial  and  error  method must  be  used to  solve  the  boundary  value  problem. In  this  paper,  an  iterative  method  of solution  for the governing differential  equations is presented. The treatment  is  limited  to  binary  permeation  systems with cocurrent or countercurrent flow  in a rigid-fiber module.  In the case of  pure  gases -as in measuring pure-gas permeabilities -it is  possible  to  extend the method to spiral-wound  modules.  In the following we  also  examine  the  effects  of permeate  pressure build-up  on  performance  of  the  module  and  describe a method  for determining  the  optimum  permeate pressures  in  single-stage  and  cascade  operations.

texts.orig: The prediction of  module  performance by the above method  requires a  prior knowledge  of  membrane permeabilities for pure gases.  Often it is necessary to determine such permeabilities from pure gas permeation through the module.  For  modules  with narrow  hollow fibers the permeate  pressure build-up  inside the fiber must  be  taken  into  account in  calculating  the permeabilities  from  the  observed  permeate  flows.  In  this section  a  method  for  determining  the  intrinsic  permeability  of  the hollow  fiber  module  is  presented.

texts.orig: In  a  practical  module  it  is  sometimes  difficult  to know  the  inside  diameter  of  the  fibers.  It  may  be possible  to  measure a  few samples but these  may  not be  representative.  Since D, occurs to the fourth power in  the  expression  for  pressure  drop,  a  small  change in D, can  make  a  large  change  in  the  performance of  the  module.  It  is  possible  to  estimate  a  value  for D, from a series of  measurements of  permeate flows at different  pressures.  Experimentally,  there  are two approaches : maintain  the  same  pressure  difference between  feed  and  permeate  outlet  and vary  the  feed pressure,  or  maintain  a  constant  feed  pressure  and vary the permeate outlet pressure.  In either  case,  one must resort to a trial-and-error procedure of  guessing a  value  for  the  inside  diameter  of the  fiber  and then  calculating  a  set  of values  of Qld from  the observed  permeate  flows as  described above. The correct  diameter  should  give  a  constant Qld irrespective  of the  measuring  pressures (assuming  no plasticizing  effects  and  no  large  holes  in  the  membrane  permitting  viscous  flow).

texts.orig: Figure 4 shows the  effect  of  the  permeate  outlet pressure on  the  permeator and  compression  costs. These  two  costs  curves  are  calculated  based  on a particular ratio  of the  unit  membrane  cost  to  the unit compression cost  (including amortization, investment  return  and  operating  cost);  the  ratio  chosen here  is  believed  to  be  what  may  be  attainable  in practice  but  the  exact  value  is  not  important  to  the argument. The  permeator  cost  generally  decreases with  decreasing  permeate  pressure  due  to  increased helium recovery per permeator module.  But this effect tends  to  diminish  a t   low  permeate  outlet  pressure. On  the  other  hand,  the  permeate  compression  cost goes  through  a  minimum  with  the  variation  of  the permeate  outlet  pressure.  At  high  permeate  outlet pressures, the compression cost decreases with decreasing pressure  due to  increased permeate concentration (or  equivalently, decreased  permeate volume per  unit volume  of helium  recovered).  But  a t   low  permeate outlet  pressures,  the compression  cost  increases  with decreasing pressure due  to increased  compression ratio. It is  clear from  these effects that the optimum permeate  outlet  pressure,  which  gives  the  minimum helium  cost (permeator  cost + compression  cost) will be  below the permeate outlet pressure corresponding  to  the  minimum  permeate  compression  cost.  The exact  location  of  the  optimum  permeate  outlet  pressure  will  depend  on  the  relative  vertical  positions of  the permeator  and  compression cost  curves which, in  turn,  are  affected  by  the  ratio  of  the  unit  membrane  cost  to  the  unit  compression  cost.  The  permeator  and compression cost curves shown  in  Figure 4 can be considered as helium cost curves corresponding  to  the  membrane/compression  cost  ratios  equal to  infinite  and  zro,  respectively.  Thus  the  lower  the compression cost, the  lower  is  the optimum  permeate outlet  pressure.

texts.orig: Optimum permeate  pressure in cascade  operation
texts.text: Optimum permeate  pressure in cascade  operation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  determination  of  the  optimum  permeate  pressure  in  each  stage  of the  cascade  is  an  important design  problem. It  is  not easily amenable  to  the sequential  optimization  method  of  dynamic  programming  due  to  the  residue  recycling.  In  the  following, we  seek  an  approximate but simplified  sequential  optimization  method applicable only  to the ideal cascade without  stripping  stages  such  as  shown  in  Figure 6. In such a  cascade,  the concentration  of  the  residue of  each  of  the  second  and  subsequent  stages  is  set equal to the feed concentration of  the preceding stage. The  optimization  method  presented  below  is  for  the determination of  the near-optimum permeate pressure in  each  stage for  given  membrane  modules,  feed-side pressures,  and  concentrations  of the  feed,  residue, and  product  streams  of the  cascade.  The  number  of stages required  will  depend  on the product  concentration  specified.  The  permeate  pressure  for  the  last stage is decided by the product concentration requirements  rather  than  the cost, which  is  generally  much smaller than the cost for any of  the preceding stages. The following  discussions are applicable to cases with or  without  permeate  pressure  drop.
texts.text: The  determination  of  the  optimum  permeate  pressure  in  each  stage  of the  cascade  is  an  important design  problem. It  is  not easily amenable  to  the sequential  optimization  method  of  dynamic  programming  due  to  the  residue  recycling.  In  the  following, we  seek  an  approximate but simplified  sequential  optimization  method applicable only  to the ideal cascade without  stripping  stages  such  as  shown  in  Figure 6. In such a  cascade,  the concentration  of  the  residue of  each  of  the  second  and  subsequent  stages  is  set equal to the feed concentration of  the preceding stage. The  optimization  method  presented  below  is  for  the determination of  the near-optimum permeate pressure in  each  stage for  given  membrane  modules,  feed-side pressures,  and  concentrations  of the  feed,  residue, and  product  streams  of the  cascade.  The  number  of stages required  will  depend  on the product  concentration  specified.  The  permeate  pressure  for  the  last stage is decided by the product concentration requirements  rather  than  the cost, which  is  generally  much smaller than the cost for any of  the preceding stages. The following  discussions are applicable to cases with or  without  permeate  pressure  drop.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flow  rates of  the  feed,  residue  and  product  of the  cascade  can  be  considered  as  known  parameters, since for a given  helium  recovery  rate  (a  usual  basis for  a  calculation)  these  flow  rates  can  be  calculated by  material  balance  from the  given  feed, residue  and product  concentrations.  Now  considering  the  two  interstage  streams between  the  first and  second  stages (cf. Figure 6 ) , the concentration  of  the  second  stage residue  must  be equal  to  that  of the  feed  to  the cascade  (no  mixing  loss) ; the  first  stage  permeate concentration is dependent only on  its permeate pressure because  the feed  and residue  concentrations  are specified  at the outset  (see the  section  of  calculation method). The  flow rates  of these two interstage streams  will  be  fixed  (by  material  balance  over  the f i r s t  stage)  once  the  first  stage  permeate  concentration  is  decided  by the permeate  pressure.  Here  it  is imoprtant  to  recognize  that  the  flow  rates  and  concentrations  of  these  two  streams will  not  be  affected by  whatever  permeate  pressures  are  chosen  for  the subsequent  stages.  This  is  because  the  state  of the final  product  is  specified  at  the  outset.  Consequently the permeator and compression  costs of  the first stage are  dependent  only  on its  permeate  pressure.  The permeate  pressure  which  minimizes  the  first  stage cost  would  be  the optimum  pressure  if  the  resulting first  stage permeate  concentration  also gave  a  minimum  combined  cost  for  the  subsequent  stages.  The last  provision,  however, is  generally  not  true.  The cost  for the  subsequent  stages always  decreases with increasing  first  stage  permeate  concentration  due  to increased  concentrations  in all  streams of  subsequent stages. But  the variation  of  the first stage  permeate pressure usually has a  greatereffect  on the cost of  the  first  stage  than  the  combined  cost  of subsequent  stages.  This  has  been  verified  by  numerous calculations for different permeation systems  and is due to the fact t h a t the membrane area and  permeate  compression  power  requirements  generally  decrease  markedly  with  the  succeeding  stages. Under  such  conditions, the  permeate  pressure  which minimizes  the  first  stage  cost  is  approximately  the optimum  pressure  (strictly speaking,  the  exact  optimum  permeate  pressure  is  slightly  less  than this pressure).  The  proof  of  this  is  by  contradiction.  If the  first  stage  permeate  pressure in  the  optimum cascade  did not minimize  the  first  stage  cost,  it would  be  possible  to  decrease  the  cost  of  the cascade by varying t h e   first  stage permeate  pressure  to decrease  the  first  stage  cost  since  the  decrease  in first  stage  cost  will  ,be greater than  the  decrease  or increase  of the  combined cost for the  subsequent stages.

Consequently the permeator and compression  costs of  the first stage are  dependent  only  on its  permeate  pressure.  The permeate  pressure  which  minimizes  the  first  stage cost  would  be  the optimum  pressure  if  the  resulting first  stage permeate  concentration  also gave  a  minimum  combined  cost  for  the  subsequent  stages.  The last  provision,  however, is  generally  not  true.  The cost  for the  subsequent  stages always  decreases with increasing  first  stage  permeate  concentration  due  to increased  concentrations  in all  streams of  subsequent stages. But  the variation  of  the first stage  permeate pressure usually has a  greatereffect  on the cost of  the  first  stage  than  the  combined  cost  of subsequent  stages.  This  has  been  verified  by  numerous calculations for different permeation systems  and is due to the fact t h a t the membrane area and  permeate  compression  power  requirements  generally  decrease  markedly  with  the  succeeding  stages. Under  such  conditions, the  permeate  pressure  which minimizes  the  first  stage  cost  is  approximately  the optimum  pressure  (strictly speaking,  the  exact  optimum  permeate  pressure  is  slightly  less  than this pressure).  The  proof  of  this  is  by  contradiction.  If the  first  stage  permeate  pressure in  the  optimum cascade  did not minimize  the  first  stage  cost,  it would  be  possible  to  decrease  the  cost  of  the cascade by varying t h e   first  stage permeate  pressure  to decrease  the  first  stage  cost  since  the  decrease  in first  stage  cost  will  ,be greater than  the  decrease  or increase  of the  combined cost for the  subsequent stages.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Once the first stage optimum  permeate  pressure  is decided,  the  concentrations  of  the  second  stage  feed and  residue  are  also  fixed  and are equal to  the  concentrations of the  first  stage  permeate  and feed, respectively. By the same reasoning  of  the first stage, the  permeate  pressure  which  minimizes  the  second stage cost is  the  optimum  second stage  permeate pressure.  The  optimum  permeate  pressure  for  subsequent  stages  can  be  similarly  determined  in  a  sequential  fashion  until  a  stage  is  reached  where  the specified  product  concentration  can  be  achieved by merely  adjusting  the  permeate  pressure.  If,  however, the  necessary  permeate  pressure  for  this  stage  to achieve  the  product  Concentration is  too  low,  it  may be  cheaper  to  optimize  the  permeate  pressure  in  this stage  as  in  the  preceding  stages  and  add  one  more stage  to  obtain  the  product  concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  above discussions  establish  the  sequence of optimization  for  permeate  pressures.  Now,  turning to  the problem of  determining the optimum permeate pressure  to  minimize  the  stage  cost  (permeator  and permeate  compression  costs),  we  have  shown  above that  for  given  feed  and  residue  concentrations  in  a single-stage operation, there always exists a  permeate pressure which minimizes the unit helium  permeation cost.  In  multistage  operation,  however,  such  a  pressure  is not  necessarily  the  optimum  one,  because the amount of  helium  permeated  through  each  stage generally varies  with  the permeate pressure  chosen.  The optimum  pressure  should  minimize  the product of the  unit  helium  permeation  cost  and  the  amount  of helium  permeated  through  this stage.  The  volume  of helium permeated through each stage per unit volume of  helium recovered in the final product is a function of the concentration  of  the  final  product  stream  and the  concentrations  of  the  feed  and  permeate  streams of  the  stage  concerned  as  follows.

texts.orig: To  determine  the  optimum  permeate  pressure  in a  stage  for  the  given  feed  and  residue  concentrations we  first calculate the unit helium permeation cost  and  volume  of  helium  permeated through  the stage  per  unit  volume  of  helium  recovered  in  the product ( U ) as functions of  permeate pressure. The permeate pressure  which  gives the minimum value of U x unit helium permeation cost is the optimum pressure  for the  stage.  We  note,  however, that  in some  cases  the  value  of U is  quite  insentitive  to the  variation  of permeate  pressure.  Under  such conditions, the permeate  pressure corresponding to  the  minimum  unit  helium  permeation  cost  is practically  the  optimum  pressure.

That this  cascade  gives  the minimum  helium  recovery cost has  been verified by  calculating  the  cost  for  other  permeate  pressures (but  with  the  same  feed,  residue  and  product  concentrations).
